project_overview.project_name: Lebanon Renewable Energy and System Reinforcement Project
project_overview.project_development_objective: To enable cleaner, more reliable and efficient grid electricity services in Lebanon
project_overview.beneficiaries: Immediate beneficiaries: EDL and LRA implementing entities. Ultimate beneficiaries: Lebanese consumers of improved electricity services, households, public institutions (schools, health facilities), businesses, and population at large through job creation and economic growth

primary_locations: Hermel, Ras Baalbek and Qaa area (solar PV plant site)
Beirut (National Control Center location at EDL headquarters)
Litani River basin (Qaraoun Dam, Joun, Awali, Markabi hydropower plants)
All eight governorates of Lebanon (for AMI rollout)
geographic_scope.coverage_type: national with focus on priority locations
geographic_scope.fragile_or_conflict_affected_areas: yes - Lebanon is classified as a Fragile, Conflict, and Violence (FCV) state. The country faces political crisis (caretaker government since May 2022), macroeconomic collapse, and spillover effects from the ongoing Middle East conflict with military operations along Lebanon's southern border escalating from low to high intensity fighting since October 2023

key_components.component_name: Component 1: Strengthening of EDL Operational and Commercial Systems
key_components.description: Sub-component 1.1 (US$25m): Construction of new National Control Center (NCC) with SCADA, EMS, telecommunications equipment, and training. Sub-component 1.2 (US$40m): Advanced Metering Infrastructure (AMI) center and Enterprise Resource Planning (ERP) system deployment for improved billing, collection, and commercial operations

key_components.component_name: Component 2: Increase of Grid-Connected Renewable Energy Supply and Network Reinforcement
key_components.description: Sub-component 2.1 (US$91m): Development of 150 MW grid-connected solar PV plant in Hermel/Ras Baalbek/Qaa area with 2-year O&M contract. Sub-component 2.2 (US$25m): Rehabilitation of three LRA hydropower plants (Joun, Awali, Markabi) and Qaraoun dam safety improvements. Sub-component 2.3 (US$54m): Transmission network reinforcement including Achrafieh substation rehabilitation, Deir Nbouh-Baalbek overhead line rehabilitation, Baalbek substation upgrade, and solar PV interconnection substation

Sub-component 3.1 (US$10m): Owner's Engineer support for EDL, business process review, capacity building, technical studies, and external audit. Sub-component 3.2 (US$5m): Owner's Engineer support for LRA, dam safety assessments, Dam Safety Panel of Experts, and capacity building
Zero funding allocation at project onset; can be triggered only in emergency circumstances to respond to natural or man-made disasters

implementation_arrangements.implementing_agency: Electricité du Liban (EDL) and Litani River Authority (LRA) as implementing entities, supported by respective Owner's Engineers (OEs). Ministry of Energy and Water (MOEW) provides strategic oversight. Project Steering Committee chaired by MOEW includes representatives from EDL, LRA, and Ministry of Finance (MOF)
implementation_arrangements.coordination_mechanisms: Project Steering Committee for strategic guidance and oversight; Project Management Teams (PMTs) established within EDL and LRA with project manager, procurement specialist, financial management specialist, and environmental & social specialists; regular coordination meetings and sector working groups led by World Bank
implementation_arrangements.procurement_approach: International competitive bidding for main contracts; World Bank Procurement Regulations (September 2023) apply; high prior review threshold due to high procurement risk; Standard Procurement Documents (SPDs) used; STEP platform for procurement planning and tracking; advance procurement method for Owner's Engineer contracts; forced labor declarations required from solar PV panel suppliers
implementation_arrangements.monitoring_and_evaluation: Semi-annual and annual monitoring by PMTs; quarterly interim financial reports; annual audited financial statements; Implementation Status & Results Reports; Mid-term Review at project midpoint; Implementation Completion and Results Report at project closure; M&E specialist within PMT; monitoring of environmental and social compliance; citizen feedback tracking through Grievance Redress Mechanism

identified_risks.risk_description: Lebanon's Parliament has not selected a new President since October 30, 2022; government continues in caretaker capacity; exacerbation of security situation due to spillover effects of ongoing Middle East conflict may impede implementation and worsen FCV situation
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Risks acknowledged as largely beyond Bank control; focus on 'no regret' investments that are critical even under protracted status quo; additional remedy clause in loan agreement to maintain leverage on reform actions

identified_risks.risk_description: Structural currency mismatch between revenues in Lebanese Pound (LBP) and costs in US dollars (~90% of costs); absence of unified exchange rate; low international reserves; Central Bank's limited ability to provide hard currency (approximately US$1 billion per year needed for 8 hours of services); lack of access to US$ may impact asset maintenance
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Government decision to allow EDL to collect electricity bills in US$ and LBP at client discretion; establishment of Cash Waterfall Mechanism (CWM) with MOF and BDL to ensure EDL's access to forex for operational continuity; operationalization of CWM by June 2025 per Letter of Electricity Sector Policy

identified_risks.risk_description: Government lacks track record of consistent implementation of electricity sector policies and reforms; critical need for consistent tariff adjustments to reflect economically justified costs; political crisis may affect timely government decisions on ERA establishment; need for adequate implementation of transmission and distribution network grid codes
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Disbursement condition on ERA commissioner appointments; additional remedy in Loan Agreement related to implementation of actions in Government's Letter of Electricity Sector Policy; combination of policy measures frontloaded before appraisal; Bank's programmatic advisory services (PASA) to inform reform agenda

identified_risks.risk_description: Project uses well-established technologies but requires detailed assessments and due diligence; construction and operational challenges possible given Lebanon's context
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Detailed assessments and due diligence conducted during preparation; Owner's Engineers hired to prepare technical specifications; O&M contracts for solar PV include capacity building; climate resilient design standards incorporated; international best practices applied

identified_risks.risk_description: EDL and LRA lack experience implementing Bank-financed projects; capacity gaps in fiduciary and environmental & social aspects; recent economic crisis has accelerated depletion of qualified human resources from public entities; limited staffing with adequate skills
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Establishment of dedicated PMTs with qualified staff and hired consultants; comprehensive Owner's Engineer support including formal and on-the-job capacity building; Component 3 provides capacity building support; hands-on Bank support and supervision; HEIS (Hands-on Expanded Implementation Support) available upon request; external auditors recruited; training provided to PMT staff

identified_risks.risk_description: High procurement risk due to market instability, price fluctuations, exchange rate volatility, complex nature of activities, limited implementing agency capacity, new to World Bank operations; narrow competition due to survival of large firms and collapse of small enterprises; contractor difficulties accessing guarantees/loans for liquidity; lengthy procurement processes
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: High prior review threshold; use of World Bank SPDs; international competitive bidding for main contracts; PMTs with procurement specialists; Owner's Engineer support for technical specifications and bidding packages; Bank may extend HEIS for procurement support; regional and international market outreach required; capacity building for procurement

identified_risks.risk_description: High FM risk due to: elevated corruption perception in country; limited civil servants availability; deterioration of local currency; informal capital controls; delays in transfers and payments; no previous Bank project experience at EDL/LRA; limited capacity of Court of Accounts for comprehensive audits; lack of integrated financial systems; EDL audit history shows disclaimer opinions and significant internal control deficiencies
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Ring-fenced project accounting system using excel spreadsheets (temporary until ERP deployed); segregated Designated Accounts in US$ at Central Bank; quarterly interim financial reports within 45 days; annual audited financial statements by independent external auditors; Project Operations Manual with detailed FM chapter; external auditors recruited within 6 months of effectiveness; OE oversight of financial progress; direct payment method largely used; EDL to establish opening balance and address audit deficiencies; internal audit function to be established

identified_risks.risk_description: Diverse stakeholder groups in electricity sector (public and private) with conflicting directions and resistance to change; complex division of responsibilities between EDL and DSPs creates challenges
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Stakeholder consultations during preparation showed overall support; alignment achieved between stakeholders on CWM; leveraging donor community to facilitate cooperation; frequent coordination meetings; regular dialogues through sector working groups; representatives from private sector, civil society, and academia consulted regularly; feedback loop for course correction

identified_risks.risk_description: Environmental risk: Substantial - civil and electromechanical works associated with temporary reversible impacts (air/noise emissions, waste generation, chemical use, water pollution, ecosystem impacts); potential PCB contamination in old transformers; large land occupation for PV plant; operational impacts including e-waste and water consumption. Social risk: Substantial - temporary predictable reversible impacts; potential child labor and forced labor in solar PV manufacturing; OHS and community health risks; SEA/SH risks; perception of exclusion from benefits; downstream populations affected by HPP operations
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA) for 150 MW PV plant; Environmental and Social Management Plans (ESMPs) for transmission line rehabilitation, NCC construction, dam maintenance; Dam Safety Report per ESS4; Labour Management Procedures; Stakeholder Engagement Plan disclosed; E&S screening conducted; qualified E&S specialists in PMTs; E&S focal points recruited; hazardous waste management plan for old transformers; climate resilient design standards; biodiversity mitigation measures; fire protection systems; vegetation management; inclusive stakeholder consultations; grievance redress mechanism

triggered_safeguard_policies: ESS 1: Assessment and Management of Environmental and Social Risks and Impacts
ESS 3: Resource Efficiency and Pollution Prevention and Management
ESS 6: Biodiversity Conservation and Sustainable Management of Living Natural Resources
ESS 10: Stakeholder Engagement and Information Disclosure
safeguards_and_social.grievance_redress_mechanism: yes - Project-level Grievance Redress Mechanism (GRM) established; communities can submit complaints to project-level mechanisms or World Bank's Grievance Redress Service (GRS); World Bank's Accountability Mechanism (AM) available including Inspection Panel and Dispute Resolution Service; Lebanon's IMPACT platform used to share project information and solicit feedback; percentage of registered citizen feedback cases (complaints, suggestions, compliments) addressed and responded to in stipulated time tracked as intermediate indicator
safeguards_and_social.gender_considerations: Project recognizes that energy poverty disproportionately affects women in Lebanon; women forced to develop time-consuming coping mechanisms; lack of decision-making power on energy expenditure; women's mental health and intra-household dynamics negatively impacted. Project will support EDL efforts to promote enabling environment for women through internships/apprenticeships; target of 30% female representation in internships/apprenticeships by SOP2; capacity building on gender diversity; outreach to universities; targeted recruitment messaging; collaboration with regional/global women in energy networks; gender gap assessment planned under SOP1
vulnerable_groups: Households in energy poverty (50.1% receiving 4 hours or less daily electricity at time of survey)
Low-income households relying on expensive diesel generators
Women disproportionately affected by energy poverty
Public institutions (schools, health facilities) dependent on grid electricity
Communities near solar PV plant sites
Workers in construction and operation phases
Populations in fragile and conflict-affected areas

fcv_relevant_details.conflict_sensitivity: Project explicitly addresses FCV context. Electricity crisis coupled with economic and social marginalization of vulnerable groups (those unable to afford private generator prices) and perceptions of grievances and injustice are identified as drivers of fragility. Failure to solve electricity crisis in socially fair manner will increase risk of social unrest, violence, and conflict. Project supports green growth in resilient and inclusive manner with focus on restoring basic services. Project aligned with World Bank's FCV strategy and MENA Regional Update 2021. Preventing and mitigating FCV challenges identified as key to progress on SDGs and promoting peace and prosperity. Project design incorporates conflict sensitivity considerations and aims to reduce fragility through service restoration
fcv_relevant_details.fragility_context: Lebanon classified as Fragile, Conflict, and Violence (FCV) state. Key fragility factors: (1) Economic and financial crisis - worst globally since mid-19th century; GDP contracted 34% (2018-2022); currency lost 98% of value; triple-digit inflation; 44% of population in five governorates identified as poor; 30% unemployment, 50% youth unemployment. (2) Political crisis - highly polarized political landscape; caretaker government since May 2022; Parliament unable to select new President since October 2022; stalled economic recovery prospects. (3) Institutional crisis - lack of accountability and transparency; concerns about efficient use of resources; exacerbated inequality and poverty. (4) Social tensions - public discontent fueled by crises; aggravated social polarization. (5) Geopolitical tensions - foreign interventions in political system; ongoing Middle East conflict with spillover effects. (6) Infrastructure collapse - electricity services collapsed from grid (1-2 hours daily in 2022, recovered to 4-5 hours by early 2023); critical barrier to economic recovery; exacerbates social inequalities. (7) Systemic banking failure - pervasive dollarized cash-based economy; limited access to credit
fcv_relevant_details.security_considerations: Military operations along Lebanon's southern border escalated from low to high intensity fighting since October 2023 due to spillover effects of ongoing Middle East conflict. This poses shock to Lebanon's economic recovery and growth prospects. Security situation may impede project implementation. Project design incorporates climate resilience and disaster risk considerations. NCC building designed with no flooding risk and climate resilient features (thermal insulation). Solar PV site not subject to flooding risk. Transmission infrastructure designed with climate resilient standards (foundations and tower design accounting for extreme wind, flooding, seismic events). Transformers equipped with cooling systems for extreme heat. Substations equipped with firefighting and fire alarm systems. Vegetation management planned to eliminate fire hazards. Dam safety improvements include monitoring instrumentation for water level changes during drought or floods
fcv_relevant_details.displacement_or_refugees: no - Project does not involve involuntary resettlement. NCC reconstruction on EDL publicly owned lands. Solar PV plant sites selected as public lands with no encroachment based on agreed criteria. Project Operations Manual includes criteria to exclude sites that may cause resettlement. Populations residing downstream of three HPPs (in operation since 1960s) not expected to be negatively impacted by rehabilitation works. However, Lebanon hosts significant refugee populations (Syrian and Palestinian) affected by electricity crisis; project benefits will extend to refugee communities through improved grid electricity services
